hook: You already know how to calculate statistics like mean, median, and standard deviation from a data set. But where does that data come from in the first place? Imagine a researcher claims that a new study 'proves' a drug cures headaches — but you later find out participants were allowed to choose whether to take the drug or a placebo. Does that change how much you trust the conclusion? The question is: how do we decide whether a study's design actually supports its conclusions?
concept: A study's design determines what kinds of conclusions are valid. A **survey** collects self-reported data from a sample without any intervention. An **observational study** watches subjects and records what happens naturally, without assigning treatments. An **experiment** deliberately assigns subjects to treatments (and a control group) so the researcher controls the key variable. Only a well-designed experiment can establish cause-and-effect; surveys and observational studies can reveal associations but not causation.
anatomy: Every study design has four structural pieces worth naming: the **population** (the entire group of interest), the **sample** (the subset actually studied), the **variable of interest** (what is being measured or observed), and the **treatment or intervention** (present only in experiments — the condition deliberately assigned by the researcher). In an experiment we also identify the **control group** (receives no treatment or a placebo) and the **treatment group** (receives the intervention). Observational studies and surveys lack an assigned treatment, which is precisely why they cannot prove causation.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Scenario: A school nurse records whether students who eat breakfast score higher on morning quizzes.}
worked_example.description_latex: We start by reading the scenario carefully to identify who is being studied and what is being measured. The population is all students; the variable of interest is quiz score; the nurse is simply recording existing behavior without changing anything about what students do.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Is a treatment assigned? } \longrightarrow \text{No. The nurse only observes.}
worked_example.description_latex: The single most important question is whether the researcher assigned subjects to conditions. Here students chose on their own whether to eat breakfast, so the nurse had no control over the key variable — this rules out calling it an experiment.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Is data collected by asking questions, or by watching behavior?} \longrightarrow \text{Watching (recording quiz scores and breakfast habits)}
worked_example.description_latex: Surveys collect self-reported responses to questions, while observational studies watch and record without intervening. Because the nurse records behavior rather than asking students to fill out a questionnaire about their habits, this is an observational study, not a survey.

We conclude this is an observational study because no treatment was assigned and data was gathered by observation rather than self-report. This means we can note an association between breakfast and quiz scores, but we cannot conclude that eating breakfast causes higher scores — a lurking variable (like sleep) could explain both.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Scenario: A company randomly assigns 200 employees to either a 4-day workweek or a standard 5-day week for 3 months, then measures productivity.}
partial_example.description_latex: Read the scenario and identify whether the researcher is assigning a condition to subjects. Notice the word 'randomly assigns' — this is your first clue about the study type.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Is a treatment deliberately assigned by the researcher?}
partial_example.description_latex: Ask yourself: did the subjects choose their own condition, or did the researcher place them into groups? Write 'yes' or 'no' and explain what the treatment is.

Because subjects were randomly assigned to treatment groups, this is a controlled experiment. Random assignment is what makes it valid to conclude that the workweek schedule (not some other difference between groups) caused any change in productivity.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{What can we conclude vs. what is out of reach?}
partial_example.description_latex: Based on the study type, state one conclusion that IS supported and one that is NOT. For example: can we generalize to all workers everywhere, or only to employees similar to those in this company?

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A radio station asks listeners to call in and vote for their favorite song. Identify the study type: survey, observational study, or experiment.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Survey (self-reported responses; no treatment assigned; voluntary response sample)}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Researchers track 500 adults over 10 years, recording their exercise habits and heart health without instructing them to change behavior. What type of study is this, and what limitation does it have?}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Observational study; it can show association between exercise and heart health but cannot prove causation because no treatment was assigned.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A teacher randomly divides her class into two groups: one group studies with background music and the other studies in silence. She then gives both groups the same test. (a) What type of study is this? (b) Identify the treatment group and the control group.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{(a) Experiment} \\ \text{(b) Treatment group: music; Control group: silence}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A voluntary online poll finds that } 78\% \text{ of respondents support a new policy. Explain why this sample is likely biased and identify the type of study. Then describe one way to redesign the study to reduce bias.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Survey with voluntary response bias (only strongly-opinionated people tend to respond).} \\ \text{Redesign: use simple random sampling to select participants rather than allowing self-selection.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A nutritionist randomly assigns 60 volunteers to one of three diets for 8 weeks and measures weight loss. She finds the mean weight loss for Diet A is } \bar{x}_A = 4.2 \text{ lbs, for Diet B is } \bar{x}_B = 6.1 \text{ lbs, and for Diet C is } \bar{x}_C = 3.8 \text{ lbs. Identify the study type, state whether a cause-and-effect conclusion is justified, and name the population and variable of interest.}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{Experiment; cause-and-effect IS justified because of random assignment.} \\ \text{Population: all adults similar to the volunteers; Variable of interest: weight loss (lbs).}

common_mistakes: Students often classify any study that collects data as a 'survey.' Remember: a survey specifically uses self-reported answers to questions; if a researcher observes or measures subjects directly without asking them to report, it is an observational study.

Students frequently conclude that an observational study proves causation because a strong association exists. Fix this by asking: 'Was a treatment randomly assigned?' — if not, confounding variables could explain the relationship, so only association (not causation) can be claimed.

Students confuse an experiment with an observational study when the word 'study' or 'research' appears in the scenario. The defining feature of an experiment is that the researcher deliberately assigns subjects to treatment and control conditions — the word 'randomly assigns' or 'given' a treatment is the key signal.
untested_variants: Identifying and explaining specific sampling methods within surveys (simple random, stratified, cluster, systematic) and how each affects bias — not addressed in the practice problems above.
Distinguishing between a blind experiment and a double-blind experiment, and explaining why blinding reduces placebo effect and experimenter bias.
Analyzing a given study to identify a specific confounding (lurking) variable by name and explaining precisely how it could account for an observed association.
Evaluating whether a sample is representative of a population and calculating or estimating the margin of error for a survey result.
